name: From Palma to s'Arenal — return via the Basilica of Santa Maria de Mallorca
name_original: De Palma a s'Arenal — vuelta por la Basílica de Santa María de Mallorca
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Palma traverse a diverse landscape, ranging from the challenging peaks of the Serra de Tramuntana mountains to serene coastal paths and expansive flatlands. The region is characterized by its varied terrain, offering both demanding ascents and gentler routes through rural areas. Cyclists can experience winding mountain roads, coastal cliffs, and open countryside. Best road cycling routes around Palma The most popular road cycling route is Soller, Puig Major, Gorg Blau and its aqueduct — tour through Sierra de Tramuntana, a difficult 65.6 miles (105.5 km) trail that takes 5 hours 22 minutes to complete. This route features significant climbs within the Serra de Tramuntana, including Puig Major. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Coll d'en Claret, Valdemossa and Coll de Sóller — circular route through the Sierra de Tramuntana, a difficult 49.8 miles (80.1 km) path. This circular route explores the mountainous terrain and picturesque villages of the Sierra de Tramuntana. Local road cyclists also love the Southern Serra de Tramuntana Loop, a 64.6 miles (104.0 km) trail leading through the dramatic Serra de Tramuntana mountains, often completed in about 5 hours 35 minutes. Road cycling around Palma is defined by the Serra de Tramuntana mountains, coastal routes, and agricultural flatlands. The network offers options for various ability levels, from challenging mountain passes to more moderate rides. The routes in Palma are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 3000 reviews. More than 110,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Palma's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Palma?
A: Palma offers a wide variety of road cycling routes, with over 450 options available on komoot. These routes cater to all levels, from moderate rides through the flatlands to challenging ascents in the Serra de Tramuntana.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling near Palma?
A: The region around Palma provides diverse terrain for road cyclists. You can find challenging climbs and winding roads in the &lt;strong&gt;Serra de Tramuntana mountain range&lt;/strong&gt;, serene coastal paths along the Paseo Marítimo, and expansive flatlands leading to rural villages and vineyards east of the city. This variety ensures options for both demanding ascents and gentler rides.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Palma?
A: Yes, Palma offers routes suitable for beginners and families. The urban cycling paths within Palma, such as the promenade along the Paseo Marítimo, are flat and well-marked, providing an easy way to explore the city. For slightly longer, gentler rides, the flatlands east of Palma offer routes through rural areas and to beautiful beaches like Es Trenc, which are generally less demanding.

Q: Where can I find challenging road cycling routes around Palma?
A: For challenging road cycling, the &lt;strong&gt;Serra de Tramuntana mountain range&lt;/strong&gt; is the primary destination. This UNESCO World Heritage site features demanding climbs, winding roads, and significant elevation gains. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1003907365/soller-puig-major-gorg-blau-and-its-aqueduct-tour-through-sierra-de-tramuntana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soller, Puig Major, Gorg Blau and its aqueduct — tour through Sierra de Tramuntana&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1003936045/southern-serra-de-tramuntana-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Southern Serra de Tramuntana Loop&lt;/a&gt; offer substantial climbs and breathtaking views for experienced cyclists.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Palma?
A: Palma's Mediterranean climate makes it suitable for road cycling almost year-round. However, the spring months (March to May) are particularly pleasant due to mild temperatures and clear days. Autumn (September to November) also offers excellent conditions. During summer (June to August), early morning rides are recommended to avoid the midday heat.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes starting from Palma?
A: Yes, there are several circular road cycling routes available. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004028058/coll-d-en-claret-valdemossa-and-coll-de-soller-circular-route-through-the-sierra-de-tramuntana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coll d'en Claret, Valdemossa and Coll de Sóller — circular route through the Sierra de Tramuntana&lt;/a&gt; is a popular option that explores the mountainous terrain and picturesque villages of the Sierra de Tramuntana in a loop format.

Q: What scenic viewpoints can I expect along road cycling routes in Palma?
A: Road cycling routes around Palma offer numerous scenic viewpoints. The &lt;strong&gt;Serra de Tramuntana&lt;/strong&gt; provides panoramic views of jagged peaks and the coastline. The ride to &lt;strong&gt;Cap de Formentor&lt;/strong&gt; is renowned for its dreamy coastal cliffs and the Mirador de sa Creueta viewpoint. Even in the flatlands, the Coll de Randa offers stunning countryside views from the Santuari de Cura.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or attractions accessible by road bike near Palma?
A: Yes, several historical landmarks and attractions are accessible. Within Palma, you can cycle past historical sites like La Seu Cathedral and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/8075216" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bellver Castle&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/996141" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Royal Palace of La Almudaina&lt;/a&gt; is also nearby. Routes through the Serra de Tramuntana often pass through picturesque villages with historical charm.

Q: Is it possible to access road cycling routes near Palma using public transport with a bike?
A: Palma has a developing public transport system, and some buses may accommodate bikes, especially those serving routes to more rural or mountainous areas. It's advisable to check specific bus routes and their bike policies in advance, particularly for services heading into the Serra de Tramuntana, as space can be limited.

Q: Are there road cycling routes with cafe stops in Palma?
A: Many road cycling routes in Palma and its surroundings feature opportunities for cafe stops. The coastal promenade offers numerous cafes, and routes through villages in the flatlands or the Serra de Tramuntana often pass through charming towns where you can find cyclist-friendly cafes to refuel and relax.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Palma?
A: The road cycling experience in Palma is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars. Reviewers frequently praise the region's diverse terrain, from challenging mountain passes to scenic coastal roads, and the well-maintained routes. The stunning natural beauty, including the dramatic landscapes of the Serra de Tramuntana and the iconic Cap de Formentor, are often highlighted as major draws.

Q: Can I find moderate difficulty road cycling routes around Palma?
A: Absolutely. While Palma is known for its challenging mountain routes, there are also many moderate options. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005264900/llucmajor-cycling-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Llucmajor Cycling Route&lt;/a&gt; offer a balanced experience with less extreme elevation changes, making them suitable for cyclists looking for a good workout without the intensity of the major mountain climbs. These routes often traverse the agricultural flatlands and rolling hills.
tips: Worth a visit, if necessary park the racing bikes in the Rapha Clubhouse and inspect them

Golden Sand stretches for miles in the Bay of Palma and scores points with fine golden sand. Well maintained as it is with nice cafés, parasols and sun loungers, it attracts a diverse crowd. But that doesn't just consist of German sunbathers and party animals. Many Spaniards and local families use the central location of the wide Can Pastilla and spend their free time and after work there. Getting there is also uncomplicated: a bus commutes to Palma and back.
If you are looking for a bit of exercise on land, you can stroll, jog or skate on the palm-lined, traffic-calmed boulevard towards the capital. In the other direction, the beach promenade leads towards Ballermann. The numbered beach cafés called Balnearios offer orientation aids. The entire beach in the Bay of Palma is divided into 15 zones, stretching from the capital to Ballermanner.

In the high season you are of course not alone at Can Pastilla, but you are also not as squeezed in as you would expect on the most obvious of all Mallorca beaches. In the low season, on the other hand, it gets pleasantly empty.
See and be seen is the motto. But for rolling in and out when the base is in El Arenal, super nice.

Wide beach with a lot of sandy space in front of the sea and with a group of palm trees that are located on the promenade that joins the beach with the center of S'Arenal. Many establishments to hang out.
On the route from San Jordi to Sa Cabaneta Sa Casa Blanca, you pass the airport directly and are often very close to the planes that are landing and taking off.

waypoints.name: Cathedral of Santa Maria of Palma (La Seu)
